content.full_text:
LAW OFFICES OF

BAGATELOS & FADEM
THE INTERNATIONAL BUILDING
601 CALIFORNIA STREET
SUITE 1801
SAN FRANCISCO, CALIFORNIA 94108

BARRY FADEM
PETER A. BAGATELOS
WES VAN WINKLE

TELEPHONE
(415) 982-7100
FAX
(415) 982-1085

May 15, 1990


Sandy Taylor
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA  95814

Dear Sandy:

     This will confirm our telephone conversation on May 10, 1990.

     I described the following facts.  My client is a candidate for office.  My client's controlled campaign committee is registered with the Secretary of State's office.  On the day that we have talked, my client's campaign committee representative received a telephone call from a person that had previously contributed a maximum $1,000 contribution to the committee during this fiscal year.  The contributor is a jeweler.  The contributor advised the committee representative that, on the prior day, he had disseminated advertisements requesting people to (1) buy a discounted watch from the jeweler containing a photo likeness of the candidate and (2) encouraging such buyers to simultaneously contribute the amount of the discount to the candidate's committee.  For example, if the watches each cost $500 normally, the jeweler might provide a discounted price of $250 and encourage the buyers to contribute the remaining $250 to the candidate committee.

     It is my understanding that the jeweler was not requiring that such contributions be made as condition of buying the watch and that his decision to engage in this activity was made by him, without any prior communication, consultation, or corporation with the candidate or the candidate's committee representatives.  It is further my understanding that he did not receive the photograph from the campaign for this purpose, although he may have received such photo as part of a campaign brochure or possibly a Christmas card that may have been mailed in the past.  In any event, it was his decision to appropriate the use of the photograph, however obtained, and to promote the sale of the discounted watches as a method of encouraging contributions to be made to the candidate's committee.

Sandy Taylor
May 15, 1990
Page Two

     Based on this information, and after checking with others in
your office, you advised me that the activities of the jeweler
would constitute independent expenditures on behalf of the
candidate's candidacy.  The independence of these expenditures
was not tainted by the telephone call to the committee
representative after the activity was commenced.  You also
concurred that the independence of the expenditures would not be
tainted if the committee representative were to again speak with
the jeweler, advise him that the independence of the
expenditures could only be maintained if there is no
consultation between the entities as to the expenditures, while
at the same time encouraging the jeweler to contact your office
for further advice regarding the reportability of the
expenditures.

     Thank you very much for your prompt response to my
question.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        [signature]
                                        Peter A. Bagatelos

PAB/map
